steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Find the y-intercept of function I: $f(x) = a(2.3)^x + b$. Set $x=0$: $f(0) = a(2.3)^0 + b = a(1) + b = a + b$. The y-intercept is $a+b$.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: Find the y-intercept of function II: $g(x) = a(2.3)^x + b$. Set $x=0$: $g(0) = a(2.3)^0 + b = a(1) + b = a + b$. The y-intercept is $a+b$.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content: Check whether each equation displays its y-intercept as a standalone constant or coefficient. In I, the y-intercept $a+b$ is a sum of two parameters — neither $a$ alone nor $b$ alone equals the y-intercept. In II, the y-intercept $a+b$ is also a sum of two parameters, so neither $a$ alone nor $b$ alone equals the y-intercept. Neither equation displays its y-intercept directly.

steps.title: Step 4
steps.content:
Evaluate each choice:
  • A: I only — I does not display $a+b$ as a single constant. Eliminated.
  • B: II only — II does not display $a+b$ as a single constant. Eliminated.
  • C: I and II — neither displays its y-intercept directly. Eliminated.
  • D: Neither I nor II — confirmed: neither equation has the y-intercept value visible as a standalone constant or coefficient. D is correct.
